en.extract:
The Transvolcanic jay (Aphelocoma ultramarina) , is a species of bird in the family Corvidae, the crows and jays. It is endemic to Mexico.


Taxonomy and systematics
What is now the species Aphelocoma ultramarina was originally described in 1825 as Corvus ultramarinus. It was long called the Mexican jay and included what are now the several subspecies of Aphelocoma wollweberi. Taxonomic systems began separating them in 2011 based on differences in plumage and morphology, millions of years of genetic divergence, and no evidence of interbreeding. Aphelocoma ultramarina was then named the Transvolcanic jay and confusingly Aphelocoma wollweberi retained the name Mexican jay.
The Transvolcanic jay has two subspecies, the nominate A. u. ultramaria (Bonaparte, 1825) and A. u. colimae (Nelson, 1899).


Description
The Transvolcanic jay is 28 to 32 cm (11 to 13 in) long and weighs 120 to 150 g (4.2 to 5.3 oz). The sexes have the same plumage though males are slightly larger than females. Adults of the nominate subspecies have a mostly dark bluish head with black lores and a pale grayish throat. Their upperparts are bluish purple and their underparts dingy grayish white that is lightest on the undertail coverts. Subspecies A. u. colimae is slightly smaller than the nominate and paler overall with less purplish on its upperparts. Both subspecies have a brown iris, a heavy, pointed black bill, and black legs and feet. Juveniles have mouse-gray upperparts with a slight blue wash, a gray to blue-green tail, grayer wings with dark brown feather ends, and a pale bill.


Distribution and habitat
The Transvolcanic jay derives its name from its range in the Transvolcanic Belt of south-central Mexico. Subspecies A. u. colimae is the more westerly of the two and has the smaller range. It is found in Jalisco and northeastern Colima. The nominate subspecies is found from southeastern Jalisco and northwestern Michoacán east to Veracruz. It inhabits montane pine and pine-oak forest in the subtropical zone. In elevation it ranges between about 900 and 3,400 m (3,000 and 11,200 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The Transvolcanic jay is a year-round resident.


Feeding
The Transvolcanic jay's diet is not known in detail but apparently is mostly the seeds of pines and oaks. It probably also eats fruits, invertebrates, and small vertebrates. It lives in flocks of up to about 20 individuals that maintain a territory. It forages on the ground, in shrubs, and in trees and caches and recovers food throughout the year.


Breeding
Nothing is known about the Transvolcanic jay's breeding biology. It is assumed to be similar to that of the Mexican jay, which see here.


Vocalization
The Transvolcanic jay's voice is very similar to that of the Mexican jay, and is described as "an upslurred grating call note, repeated singly, doubled or in series". The grating appears to decrease from the beginning to the end of each note.


Status
The IUCN has assessed the Transvolcanic jay as being of Least Concern. It has a very large range; its estimated population of at least 50,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered fairly common though "colimae [is] at risk because its small range is undergoing extensive habitat clearance for agriculture and timber".


References

The '''{{sic|hide=y|Transvolcanic|reason=This is a proper name, a reference to the Transvolcanic Belt, so capitalize it.}} jay''' ('''''Aphelocoma ultramarina''''') , is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Corvidae]], the crows and jays. It is [[endemic]] to Mexico.<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/crows/ |title=Jayshrike, shrikes, crows, mudnesters, melampittas, Ifrit, birds-of-paradise |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=9 December 2025 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

What is now the species ''Aphelocoma ultramarina'' was [[species description|originally described]] in 1825 as ''Corvus ultramarinus''.<ref>{{cite journal | last=Bonaparte |first=Charles Lucien | date= 1825 |title=Descriptions of two new species of Mexican Birds | journal=Journal of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia | volume=IV part II |pages=387–389 |language=English | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/24655505 |access-date=December 15, 2025 }}</ref> It was long called the Mexican jay and included what are now the several subspecies of ''[[Aphelocoma wollweberi]]''. Taxonomic systems began separating them in 2011 based on differences in plumage and morphology, millions of years of genetic divergence, and no evidence of interbreeding. ''Aphelocoma ultramarina'' was then named the Transvolcanic jay and confusingly ''Aphelocoma wollweberi'' retained the name Mexican jay.<ref name=AOU1998>{{cite book | last =<!--Not stated--> | first =<!--Not stated--> |  title =Check-list of North American Birds | publisher =American Ornithologists' Union |edition =7th | date =1998 | location =Washington, D.C. | pages =447 }}</ref><ref name=52Supp>R. Terry Chesser, Richard C. Banks, F. Keith Barker, Carla Cicero, Jon L. Dunn, Andrew W. Kratter, Irby J. Lovette, Pamela C. Rasmussen, J. V. Remsen, Jr., James D. Rising, Douglas F. Stotz, and Kevin Winker. "Fifty-second supplement to the American Ornithologists' Union ''Check-list of North American Birds''". ''The Auk'' 2011, vol. 128:605 retrieved October 22, 2025</ref>

The Transvolcanic jay has two subspecies, the [[Nominate subspecies|nominate]] ''A. u. ultramaria'' ([[Charles Lucien Bonaparte|Bonaparte]], 1825) and ''A. u. colimae'' ([[Edward William Nelson|Nelson]], 1899).<ref name=IOC15.1/>

==Description==

The Transvolcanic jay is {{convert|28|to|32|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|120|to|150|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage though males are slightly larger than females. Adults of the nominate subspecies have a mostly dark bluish head with black [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]] and a pale grayish throat. Their upperparts are bluish purple and their underparts dingy grayish white that is lightest on the undertail [[coverts]]. Subspecies ''A. u. colimae'' is slightly smaller than the nominate and paler overall with less purplish on its upperparts. Both subspecies have a brown iris, a heavy, pointed black bill, and black legs and feet. Juveniles have mouse-gray upperparts with a slight blue wash, a gray to blue-green tail, grayer wings with dark brown feather ends, and a pale bill.<ref name=TRJA-BOW>Marzluff, J. and J. S. Marks (2020). Transvolcanic Jay (''Aphelocoma ultramarina''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.mexjay3.01 retrieved December 25, 2025</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The Transvolcanic jay derives its name from its range in the [[Transvolcanic Belt]] of south-central Mexico. Subspecies ''A. u. colimae'' is the more westerly of the two and has the smaller range. It is found in [[Jalisco]] and northeastern [[Colima]]. The nominate subspecies is found from southeastern Jalisco and northwestern [[Michoacán]] east to [[Veracruz]].<ref name=TRJA-BOW/> It inhabits montane pine and [[Pine-oak forest|pine-oak]] forest in the subtropical zone. In elevation it ranges between about {{convert|900|and|3400|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=52Supp/><ref name=TRJA-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The Transvolcanic jay is a year-round resident.<ref name=52Supp/>

===Feeding===

The Transvolcanic jay's diet is not known in detail but apparently is mostly the seeds of pines and oaks. It probably also eats fruits, invertebrates, and small vertebrates. It lives in flocks of up to about 20 individuals that maintain a territory. It forages on the ground, in shrubs, and in trees and caches and recovers food throughout the year.<ref name=TRJA-BOW/>

===Breeding===

Nothing is known about the Transvolcanic jay's breeding biology. It is assumed to be similar to that of the Mexican jay, which see [[Mexican jay#Ecology|here]].<ref name=TRJA-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Aphelocoma-ultramarina |species=the Transvolcanic jay}}
===Vocalization===

The Transvolcanic jay's voice is very similar to that of the Mexican jay, and is described as "an upslurred grating call note, repeated singly, doubled or in series". The grating appears to decrease from the beginning to the end of each note.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/ornith-notes/JN100211 |title=Notes on the vocalizations of Mexican Jay (''Aphelocoma ultramarina'') |last=Boesman |first=Peter F. D. |date=2016 |website=Birds of the World |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |access-date=December 15, 2025}}</ref>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the Transvolcanic jay as being of Least Concern. It has a very large range; its estimated population of at least 50,000 mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered fairly common though "''colimae'' [is] at risk because its small range is undergoing extensive habitat clearance for agriculture and timber".<ref name=TRJA-BOW/>

zh.extract:
灰胸丛鸦（学名：Aphelocoma ultramarina），是鸦科丛鸦属的一种，分布于墨西哥和美国。全球活动范围约为572,000平方千米。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
灰胸丛鸦的平均体重约为128.3克。栖息地包括亚热带或热带的湿润低地林和亚热带或热带的湿润山地林。


参考文献

Aphelocoma ultramarina. BirdLife.org.   [2011-06-27]. （原始内容存档于2016-03-04）.
